mappings.rationaleAI: Washington's leadership of the Continental Army and decisive victory at Yorktown cover the War of Independence (A.1.a.ii). His presidency, Constitutional Convention role, and policy precedents map to the strengthening of national government and development of national policies (A.1.b.i–ii).
Colonial grievances and the Declaration of Independence (A.1.a.i), the War of Independence with French support (A.1.a.ii), and the Articles of Confederation (A.1.a.iii) are comprehensively covered.

Lincoln's election, the Republican Party's emergence (A.3.b), the Civil War military strategy (A.3.c.i–ii), and the Emancipation Proclamation and moves toward emancipation (A.3.c.iii) are directly covered.

The broad sweep of US history from the Revolution through constitutional republic, westward expansion, the Civil War, industrialization, and World Wars covers national formation (A) and post-1865 development (B).

Plantation labor systems and abolition movements connect to cotton and slavery in American commerce (A.2.a.ii) and the economic and psychological bases of slavery and the Abolitionist movement (A.3.a).

US neutrality and submarine warfare (B.3.c.i), US entry and military mobilization (B.3.c.ii), and Wilson's policies at the Paris Peace Conference and Senate rejection of the Treaty of Versailles (B.3.c.iii) are directly covered.
The emergence of the US as an imperial power through the Spanish-American War, Open Door policy, and Panama Canal Zone acquisition (B.3.a.i–iii) directly represents American imperialism.

Canada's British colonial rule, French and English settler conflicts, Confederation (1867), westward expansion, and development under Liberal governments comprehensively covers Canada from 1763 to 1920 (C.1–3).
Marx's theories of class struggle and labor economics connect to the emergence of national labor union organizations, strikes, and the antilabor stance of government (B.2.e).
Spain's defeat in the Spanish-American War of 1898 led to US suzerainty over Cuba and acquisition of the Philippines and Puerto Rico (B.3.a.i).
The Mexican War (1846–48) and annexation of California and New Mexico (A.2.d), and Wilson's intervention in Mexican affairs (B.3.b.iii) are directly covered.
Napoleon's sale of the Louisiana territory to Jefferson in 1803 connects to Jefferson's administration and the Louisiana Purchase (A.1.b.iii).
British colonial administration of Canada (C.1) and Canada's involvement in Britain's imperialist policies including the South African War (C.3.c.ii) connect to the British Empire's influence.
The Rocky Mountains connect to the development of the West (B.2.b), including westward expansion of railroads and settlement patterns.

Enlightenment liberalism and natural rights theory formed the ideological foundation for political and economic opposition to Britain's taxation policies and the Declaration of Independence (A.1.a.i).
US acquisition of the Philippines in 1898 through the Spanish-American War directly maps to the emergence of the US as an imperial power (B.3.a.i).
The broad sweep of North American history from European colonization through the American Revolution, westward expansion, industrialization, and World Wars covers the full scope of this section.
North America's geography, European colonization, American Revolution, and westward expansion provide broad context for the entire section on US and Canadian development.
French military support for the American colonies during the War of Independence (A.1.a.ii) was a decisive factor in the American victory.
The modern era's spread of nationalism, liberalism, and industrialization provides broad context for US national formation (A) and transformation (B).
Protestant religious attitudes and revivals connect to the 'Age of Reform' and diverse religious attitudes of the 1830–50 period (A.2.c).
The 'Open Door' policy and US armed intervention in China (1900) directly covers American foreign policy toward China (B.3.a.ii).
Human rights principles connect to the Abolitionist movement and slavery debate (A.3.a), freedmen's civil rights under Reconstruction (B.1.a), and Jim Crow legislation and the black response (B.1.c).
Business structures including trusts and holding companies connect to the industrialization of the American economy and development of a legal climate favorable to big business (B.2.c.ii).
